title: William Friedkin Meets a Real-Life ‘Exorcist’ | The Big Picture (Ep. 61)
format: Sean Fennessey sits down with William Friedkin, who made a documentary about a real Vatican exorcism and insists his own Exorcist is still 99.99% fiction.

segments.heading: William Friedkin on 'The Devil and Father Amorth'

films.title: The Devil and Father Amorth
films.note: Friedkin's documentary, shot alone with a small handheld camera. It shows the ninth exorcism performed on a woman named Christina by the Vatican's Father Gabriel Amorth. He took the footage to neurosurgeons and psychiatrists afterward, and none of them could explain it.

films.title: The Exorcist
films.note: His most famous film, and the reason Amorth agreed to meet him at all: the priest had written that the special effects were over the top but the movie helped people understand his work. Friedkin insists it's fiction, loosely drawn from a 1949 case, and that the head-spinning and levitation were invented for the screen, not real exorcism symptoms.

films.title: Sorcerer
films.note: The film he says came closest to his original vision and one of the few of his own he can still watch. He'd recently seen it projected for the first time and called it an amazing experience.

films.title: To Live and Die in L.A.
The People vs. Paul Crump
films.note: His first film, a documentary about a man on death row in Chicago. He says it saved the man's life.

films.title: A Quiet Place
films.note: Something he'd recently caught and called terrific, and different from the rest of the horror genre.

films.title: Get Out
films.note: His pick for the last great thing he'd seen: original premise, constant dread, and a lead he identified with.

films.title: Lawrence of Arabia
films.note: One of the films he says young viewers now watch on an iPad without minding. He thinks that misses how it was meant to be seen.

films.title: Citizen Kane
films.note: One of the older films he watches over and over.

films.title: The Band Wagon
films.note: The one he calls his peak, directed by Vincente Minnelli with Fred Astaire and Cyd Charisse. He calls watching Astaire a reason to live.

films.title: The Verdict
films.note: A Paul Newman film he still rewatches regularly.

films.title: The Parallax View
films.note: Alan Pakula's film, which he says is still as relevant now as it was in the '70s.

To Live and Die in L.A..title: To Live and Die in L.A.
